story:
We are the left behind—the ones who didn’t make the cut when the Ark rose from the dying Earth. They called it a lottery, but somehow every billionaire, influencer, celebrity, and politician found a seat. A few ordinary people were “randomly selected,” too, just enough to make the lie look true. Families broke on that blade: stay together and die, or split and gamble on survival. My mother chose the ship. I wasn’t chosen. They promised to return. We knew they wouldn’t.

When the sun began to fail and swell, its pull warped the world. One side baked under constant light; the other fell into endless night where nothing grows. Then the Light Stalkers came, drawn to brightness like moths to a red, pulsing wound in the sky. We hid in the bones of the old city—ruined subway tunnels where the Stalkers won’t go. Light is their strength; shadow is their burn.

Rogue leads us. Maybe he was a soldier once, maybe a teacher; he knows the old things and how to keep us breathing. Supplies are low. A scout must go topside. Eyes fall to me. I’m small, quick, good at shadows. Lillin, seven years old and hungry to help, begs to come. I let him wait at the shadow mouth—the place where safety ends—his job to charge our glow stones in the weak sun and run at the first hint of a hiss.

Above ground, the light is flat and thin but relentless. We live on what we can coax from the broken earth. Rogue planted orange trees in scattered patches and taught me why: our bodies need vitamin C, or we get sick and die. We seed leafy greens and climbing beans wherever there’s a chance and never take more than we need. Today I need fruit and time. I crawl through collapsed walls, pockets stuffed with damp, loamy mud scooped from a mossy crawlspace—the kind of fuel that burns slow and, more important, smothers light.

The orange grove stands in full sun, the most dangerous place to be. I dash tree to tree, satchel filling, heart steady—until the hiss. I climb, leaves hiding my breath. A Stalker steps into view, bronze-scaled, long-armed, its straps set with glow stones that shine warmer than the sun. It turns its head to listen. Another hiss answers. The second one slides under my tree, bringing its own light into the shade. Shade is my only weapon. Mud is my match.

I wait. Then I throw. One glow stone on the shoulder goes dark under mud, and the creature shrieks as the shadow bites. Another throw, another stone smothered; fire crawls over its scales. It collapses, burning. The other Stalker watches, then does something I don’t expect: it plucks oranges and bursts them over the dead one’s mud-caked straps, washing the muck from the last dim stones before the shadow can spread farther. Then it tosses me an orange, then tears off one of its own glow stones and throws that too. A trade. A lesson.

Shaking, I retrieve the dead one’s stones from the smoking wreck and keep my last handful of mud ready. The living Stalker doesn’t follow. It nods—if it’s a nod—and walks away. I slip back through the seams of shadow to Lillin, to Rogue, to the lives we’re stitching from scraps.

We may be the only humans left here, but we are not the only people on this sun-scarred world. They are not just monsters. They are something else—neighbors, rivals, maybe allies. The Ark is gone. The promises were lies. What happens next is up to us.
